title: Töltött káposzta
subtitle: Hungarian stuffed cabbage in paprika and sauerkraut
excerpt: Cabbage rolls filled with pork and rice, braised on a bed of sauerkraut with smoked sausage and paprika. Richer and smokier than its Polish counterpart, a slow-cooked winter dish that improves with reheating.
sourceNotes: Töltött káposzta (stuffed cabbage) is one of the central dishes of Hungarian cooking and is common across Central and Eastern Europe in related forms. The Hungarian version is distinguished by the paprika in the filling and sauce, the sauerkraut bed on which the rolls braise, and the smoked sausage (kolbász) added to the pot. The dish is traditionally made in large quantities for winter celebrations and tastes noticeably better on the second or third day.

content.text: Töltött káposzta is related to Polish gołąbki, but it is a different dish. Where gołąbki braise in a tomato sauce, the Hungarian version sits on a bed of sauerkraut surrounded by smoked sausage and braised in paprika-seasoned stock. The sauerkraut becomes the sauce: it softens and mingles with the pork juices, the paprika, and the fat from the sausage over two hours of slow cooking.

Use a large, wide pot with a lid, the rolls need to sit in a single layer on the sauerkraut so they braise evenly. The final dish should be moist and rich with reddish cooking liquid, not dry. Soured cream at the table, not in the pot.
Blanch the cabbage and make the rolls

Bring a large pot of salted water to the boil. Cut a deep X into the base of the {{cabbage-white}} and lower it in. Peel off softened outer leaves as they loosen, 12 to 14 leaves total. Pat dry and trim the thick central rib. Mix the {{pork-mince}}, {{long-grain-rice}}, {{onion}}, {{eggs}}, 1 tablespoon {{paprika-sweet}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper-ground}}. Place a heaped tablespoon of filling near the base of each leaf, fold the sides in, and roll firmly.

Heat the oven to 170°C. Fry the {{lardons}} in the casserole dish on the hob until the fat begins to render. Add the {{sauerkraut}} and stir. Mix the {{stock-chicken}}, remaining {{paprika-sweet}}, and {{tomato-puree}} and pour in. Add the {{smoked-pork-sausage}} and {{bay-leaves}}. Arrange the rolls seam-side down on top of the sauerkraut in a single layer. Cover tightly and bake for 90 minutes to 2 hours until the rice in the rolls is fully cooked. The sauerkraut should be very soft and the cooking liquid reddish-brown. Serve with {{soured-cream}} spooned over at the table.

items.fix: Add 100 ml more hot stock, re-cover, and bake for a further 20 to 30 minutes. Check by pressing a roll. It should feel yielding throughout with no firm centre
items.cause: Too little stock, or rolls packed too tightly

items.fix: Cover the casserole tightly with foil first, then the lid. Check after 60 minutes and add stock if needed
items.cause: Lid not sealing well, or starting stock too low

items.fix: A pinch of sugar stirred into the braising liquid before baking helps. Some families rinse half the sauerkraut and use the un-rinsed half for flavour
items.cause: Very acidic sauerkraut, not enough balancing sweetness from the meat and paprika
items.symptom: Sauerkraut too sour in the finished dish

content.text: Töltött káposzta is a winter and celebration dish in Hungary. It is made for Christmas, for New Year, and for any large family gathering where a pot of something that feeds many people is needed. The traditional version uses a whole head of sauerkraut and enough rolls to fill a large stockpot. In Hungary it is said that the dish is not ready to eat until the third reheating, the sauerkraut, the paprika, and the meat fat slowly becoming one thing over successive days.
